VISA APPROVAL PREDICTIONS¶

Problem Statement¶

Bussiness Context¶

Business communities in the United States are facing high demand for human resources, but one of the constant challenges is identifying and attracting the right talent, which is perhaps the most important element in remaining competitive. Companies in the United States look for hard-working, talented, and qualified individuals both locally as well as abroad.

The Immigration and Nationality Act (INA) of the US permits foreign workers to come to the United States to work on either a temporary or permanent basis. The act also protects US workers against adverse impacts on their wages or working conditions by ensuring US employers' compliance with statutory requirements when they hire foreign workers to fill workforce shortages. The immigration programs are administered by the Office of Foreign Labor Certification (OFLC).

OFLC processes job certification applications for employers seeking to bring foreign workers into the United States and grants certifications in those cases where employers can demonstrate that there are not sufficient US workers available to perform the work at wages that meet or exceed the wage paid for the occupation in the area of intended employment.

Objective¶

In FY 2016, the OFLC processed 775,979 employer applications for 1,699,957 positions for temporary and permanent labor certifications. This was a nine percent increase in the overall number of processed applications from the previous year. The process of reviewing every case is becoming a tedious task as the number of applicants is increasing every year.

The increasing number of applicants every year calls for a Machine Learning based solution that can help in shortlisting the candidates having higher chances of VISA approval. OFLC has hired the firm EasyVisa for data-driven solutions. You as a data scientist at EasyVisa have to analyze the data provided and, with the help of a classification model:

  1. Facilitate the process of visa approvals.
  2. Recommend a suitable profile for the applicants for whom the visa should be certified or denied based on the drivers that significantly influence the case status.

Data Description¶

The data contains the different attributes of the employee and the employer. The detailed data dictionary is given below.

case_id: ID of each visa application

continent: Information of continent the employee

education_of_employee: Information of education of the employee

has_job_experience: Does the employee has any job experience? Y = Yes; N = No

requires_job_training: Does the employee require any job training? Y = Yes; N = No

no_of_employees: Number of employees in the employer’s company

yr_of_estab: Year in which the employer’s company was established

region_of_employment: Information of foreign workers and region of employment in the US

prevailing_wage: Average wage paid to similarly employed workers in a specific occupation in the area of intended employment.

unit_of_wage: Unit of prevailing wage. Values include Hourly, Weekly, Monthly, and Yearly.

full_time_position: Is the position of work full-time? Y = Full-Time Position; N = Part-Time Position

case_status: Flag indicating if the Visa was certified or denied

What is prevailing_wage¶

prevailing_wage: Average wage paid to similarly employed workers in a specific occupation in the area of intended employment. The purpose of the prevailing wage is to ensure that the foreign worker is not underpaid compared to other workers offering the same or similar service in the area of employment.

Importing necessary libraries¶

In [ ]:
# Libraries to help with reading and manipulating data
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Libaries to help with data visualization
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# To tune model, get different metric scores, and split data
from sklearn.metrics import (
    f1_score,
    accuracy_score,
    recall_score,
    precision_score,
    confusion_matrix,
    roc_auc_score,
    ConfusionMatrixDisplay,
)
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split, StratifiedKFold, cross_val_score

# To be used for data scaling and one hot encoding
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler, MinMaxScaler, OneHotEncoder, LabelEncoder

# To impute missing values
from sklearn.impute import SimpleImputer
from sklearn import metrics

# To oversample and undersample data
from imblearn.over_sampling import SMOTE
from imblearn.under_sampling import RandomUnderSampler

# To do hyperparameter tuning
from sklearn.model_selection import RandomizedSearchCV

# To define maximum number of columns to be displayed in a dataframe
pd.set_option("display.max_columns", None)

# To supress scientific notations for a dataframe
pd.set_option("display.float_format", lambda x: "%.3f" % x)

# To help with model building
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier
from sklearn.ensemble import (
    AdaBoostClassifier,
    GradientBoostingClassifier,
    RandomForestClassifier,
    BaggingClassifier,
)
from xgboost import XGBClassifier
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression

# To suppress scientific notations
pd.set_option("display.float_format", lambda x: "%.3f" % x)

# To supress warnings
import warnings

warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

In [ ]:
# Convert the binary columns to integers using replace
df['full_time_position'] = df['full_time_position'].replace({'Y': 1, 'N': 0}).astype(int)
df['case_status'] = df['case_status'].replace({'Certified': 1, 'Denied': 0}).astype(int)
df['requires_job_training'] = df['requires_job_training'].replace({'Y': 1, 'N': 0}).astype(int)
df['has_job_experience'] = df['has_job_experience'].replace({'Y': 1, 'N': 0}).astype(int)

# Convert other object columns to category dtype
df['unit_of_wage'] = df['unit_of_wage'].astype('category')
df['region_of_employment'] = df['region_of_employment'].astype('category')
df['continent'] = df['continent'].astype('category')
df['education_of_employee'] = df['education_of_employee'].astype('category')

# Display the data types to confirm the changes
print(df.info())

  • As expected, we can not draw any information from statistical summary on columns has_job_experience, requires_job_training, full_time_position, case_status as these are object varaibles converted into integer with just yes or no output.
  • Prevailing_wage data is shared differently on hourly, weekly, monthly and yearly basis. As the units are different, from statistical summary, we can not draw any meaningful insights.
  • no_of_employees: From the above statistical data, majority of firms who filed for Visa (i.e 75%) have less than 3500 employees. But there 25% of firms whose employee count is huge with max employee in a firm being around 6Lakhs.
  • We also observe there are negative employee count which is later treated.
  • Yr_of_establishment we see majority of firms are established before 2005. Around 25% of firms are established between 2005 to 2016.

Univariant Analysis¶

In [ ]:
# Defining the plotsize

In [ ]:
# Defining the plotsize
plt.figure(figsize = (20, 4)) # Adjusted figsize for a single row

categorical_features = df.select_dtypes(include = ['category']).columns #for selecting categorical dataytpes from all features
print(categorical_features)
for i, feature in enumerate(categorical_features):
  plt.subplot(1,4,i+1)   # assigning a subplot to the main plot, adjusted for a single row (1 row, 4 columns)
  plt.title(feature)
  sns.countplot(x=data[feature]) # plotting countplot with categories on x-axis (default)
  plt.xticks(rotation=45, ha='right') # Rotate x-axis labels for better readability

plt.tight_layout() # add spacing between plots
plt.show() # to show the plot

Index(['continent', 'education_of_employee', 'region_of_employment',
       'unit_of_wage'],
      dtype='object')

Summary of univariant Analysis:¶

  • We see a large number of applicants having experience and requires no training. Large number of applicants looking full time oppurtunity.
  • The number of certified people are double than the denied applicants.
  • There are a lot of outliers on number_of_employees, prevailing_wage, yr_of_establishment.
  • Prevailing wage is right skewed.
  • yr_of_estab is left skewed. We see more number of companies established around year 2000.
  • Majority of applications are recieved from Asia followed by North America and Europe.
  • We see majority of applicants are atleast Bachelor's.
  • It is intuitive that more applicants wage type is Yearly as more applicants seek for full time employment.

Bivariant Analysis:¶

In [ ]:
# Let us further check the correleation between variables using heatmap.
plt.figure(figsize = (15,10))

#select only numeric columns before calculating correlation
numeric_features = df.select_dtypes(include = ['float64', 'int64']).columns

#plot the heatmap
sns.heatmap(df[numeric_features].corr(), annot = True, vmin =-1, vmax=1, fmt=".2f", cmap="Spectral")
plt.show()

Correlation with Case_status(target) feature¶

In [ ]:
# Define the list of categorical features to analyze against 'case_status'
cols_for_bivariate = ['continent', 'education_of_employee', 'has_job_experience', 'requires_job_training', 'full_time_position', 'region_of_employment', 'unit_of_wage']

# Plot count plots for each categorical feature against 'case_status'
plt.figure(figsize=(15, 10)) # Define the size of the figure

for i, col in enumerate(cols_for_bivariate):
    plt.subplot(3, 3, i + 1) # Assign a subplot in the main plot
    sns.countplot(data=df, x=col, hue='case_status') # Create a count plot, split by 'case_status'
    plt.title(f'{col} vs. case_status') # Add a title to the subplot
    plt.xlabel(col) # Set the x-axis label
    plt.ylabel('Count') # Set the y-axis label
    plt.legend(title='case_status', loc='upper left') # Add a legend

plt.tight_layout() # Adjust layout to prevent overlapping
plt.show() # Display the plot
In [ ]:
# we shall plot pair plot against the target variable case_status.
# This helps visualize relation between numerical varaibles and case_status

plt.figure(figsize=(18,10))
# Removed binary integer columns from corr_vars as pairplot with kde is not suitable for them
corr_vars = ['no_of_employees', 'yr_of_estab', 'prevailing_wage'] # numerical values to get correlation details

sns.pairplot(data = df, vars =corr_vars , hue = 'case_status', diag_kind='kde', corner = True)  # gives pairplot. This is used to get relationship between multiple features.
plt.show(); # to display plot

<Figure size 1800x1000 with 0 Axes>

# Calculate the correlation matrix
correlation_matrix = df_encoded.corr()

# Plot the heatmap

sns.heatmap(correlation_matrix, annot = True, vmin =-1, vmax=1, fmt=".2f", cmap="Spectral")  #heapmap after onehot-encoding

plt.show()

Summary of Bivariant Analysis¶

  • Surprisingly, there no strong correlation between the features.
  • If the applicant doesnot require job_training it is more likely to be certified compared to applicants that require job training.
  • Similarly, if the applicant is looking for full_time_position, it is more likely to be certified compared to other applicants.
  • The higher the applicant is qualified, the lesser is the rejection ratio.
  • We observe very less number of applications were received for 'Island' type of region of employment.
  • From the pair plot, applications from establishments with employees less than 2Lakhs are more certified.
  • Case status has slightly positive correlation with has_job_experience, education_of_employee_Master's, and unit_of_wage_Year.
  • Prevailing_wage and unit_of_wage_Year are having highest positive correlation.
  • continent North_America, Europe and Asia has strong negative correlation which is intuitive.

Data Processing¶

Feature Engineering¶

  • We have already treated number_of_employees for negative values.
  • We still see a huge range of values for yr_of_establishment, prevailing_wage and no_of_employees.
In [ ]:
# check the count per each unique year.
print(df['yr_of_estab'].value_counts())

yr_of_estab
1998    1134
2005    1051
2001    1017
2007     994
1999     870
        ... 
1820       6
1846       4
1822       4
1810       3
1824       2
Name: count, Length: 199, dtype: int64
  • We see a large range of values for yr_of_estab column.
  • We will split all the data into 3 categories so that it can be easily processed.
In [ ]:
# categorizising the yr_of_estab into 3 categories
df["establishment_period"] = pd.qcut(
    df["yr_of_estab"],
    q=[0, 0.25, 0.5, 1], # Using approximate quantiles for 3 bins
    labels=["Old", "Mid", "New"] #label the categories as Old, Mid, New
)
print(df['establishment_period'].value_counts()) #check the number of values in each category

establishment_period
New    12473
Mid     6535
Old     6472
Name: count, dtype: int64
  • Similary for no_of_employees features, we fit all the feature data of no_of_employees into 5 buckets
  • We are dividing employees in the establishment into 5 categories as below.
In [ ]:
# Define the bin edges for no_of_employees
employee_bins = [0, 100, 1000, 10000, 100000, df['no_of_employees'].max()]

# Define the labels for the bins
employee_labels = ["micro_scale", "mini_scale", "small_scale", "mid_scale", "large_scale"]

# Categorize no_of_employees into bins
df["establishment_type"] = pd.cut(
    df["no_of_employees"],
    bins=employee_bins,
    labels=employee_labels,
    right=True, # Include the right edge of the bin
    include_lowest=True # Include the lowest value in the first bin
)

# Print the value counts of the new categorical column
print(df['establishment_type'].value_counts())

establishment_type
small_scale    17803
mini_scale      5675
mid_scale       1165
micro_scale      566
large_scale      271
Name: count, dtype: int64
  • There are multiple usnits for wage like lourly, biweekly, monthly, yearly.
  • It will be difficult to compare wages if all of them are not on the same units.
  • Hence converting hourly, biweekly, monthly wage into yearly wage.
In [ ]:
# now convert prevailing_wage into same unit as yearly wage.
# Conversion dictionary
wage_multiplier = {
    "Hour": 2080,    # assuming 40 hours/week * 52 weeks
    "Week": 52,      # no of weeks per year
    "Bi-Weekly": 26, # no of bi-Weeklys in an year
    "Month": 12,     # no of months in an year
    "Year": 1        # years
}

# Convert wages to yearly
df['yearly_wage'] = df.apply(lambda x: x['prevailing_wage'] * wage_multiplier.get(x['unit_of_wage'], 1), axis=1)
df.head() #check the head after adding new columns.
Out[ ]:
continent education_of_employee has_job_experience requires_job_training no_of_employees yr_of_estab region_of_employment prevailing_wage unit_of_wage full_time_position case_status establishment_period establishment_type yearly_wage
0 Asia High School 0 0 14513 2007 West 592.203 Hour 1 0 New mid_scale 1231782.032
1 Asia Master's 1 0 2412 2002 Northeast 83425.650 Year 1 1 New small_scale 83425.650
2 Asia Bachelor's 0 1 44444 2008 West 122996.860 Year 1 0 New mid_scale 122996.860
3 Asia Bachelor's 0 0 98 1897 West 83434.030 Year 1 0 Old micro_scale 83434.030
4 Africa Master's 1 0 1082 2005 South 149907.390 Year 1 1 New small_scale 149907.390

  • There are no missing values.
  • We shall create a copy of the data and drop the treated features
In [ ]:
# creating a copy of data
data_bkp = df.copy()

#Now drop the yr_of_estab, no_of_employees, Prevailing_wage, unit_of_wage columns
df.drop(['yr_of_estab', 'no_of_employees', 'prevailing_wage', 'unit_of_wage'], axis=1, inplace=True)

#check the datatypes of remaining ones
df.info()

<class 'pandas.core.frame.DataFrame'>
RangeIndex: 25480 entries, 0 to 25479

---  ------                 --------------  -----   
 0   continent              25480 non-null  category
 1   education_of_employee  25480 non-null  category
 2   has_job_experience     25480 non-null  int64   
 3   requires_job_training  25480 non-null  int64   
 4   region_of_employment   25480 non-null  category
 5   full_time_position     25480 non-null  int64

 7   establishment_period   25480 non-null  category

dtypes: category(5), float64(1), int64(4)

Data Preparation for Modeling¶

In [ ]:
X = df.drop(["case_status"], axis=1) #drop the target feature on X axis

# Label certified as 1 and 0 otherwise
y = df["case_status"].apply(lambda x: 1 if x == "certified" else 0)
In [ ]:
# We are splitting the data before encoding to avoid any possible data leakage.

# Define X and y using the already processed df DataFrame
X = df.drop(["case_status"], axis=1) #drop the target feature on X axis

# Use the already integer-encoded case_status column as the target
y = df["case_status"]

# Splitting data into training, validation and test set:
# first we split data into 2 parts, say temporary and test
X_temp, X_test, y_temp, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=1, stratify=y
)

# then we split the temporary set into train and validation
X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val = train_test_split(
    X_temp, y_temp, test_size=0.25, random_state=1, stratify=y_temp
)

print(X_train.info())
print('-' * 80)
print(X_train.shape, X_val.shape, X_test.shape)

<class 'pandas.core.frame.DataFrame'>
Index: 15288 entries, 5008 to 21791
Data columns (total 9 columns):
 #   Column                 Non-Null Count  Dtype   
---  ------                 --------------  -----   
 0   continent              15288 non-null  category
 1   education_of_employee  15288 non-null  category
 2   has_job_experience     15288 non-null  int64   
 3   requires_job_training  15288 non-null  int64   
 4   region_of_employment   15288 non-null  category
 5   full_time_position     15288 non-null  int64   
 6   establishment_period   15288 non-null  category
 7   establishment_type     15288 non-null  category
 8   yearly_wage            15288 non-null  float64 
dtypes: category(5), float64(1), int64(3)
memory usage: 672.8 KB
None
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
(15288, 9) (5096, 9) (5096, 9)
In [ ]:
# We need to encode the categorical values in our train, validation and test data

cat_cols = X_train.select_dtypes(include='category').columns
le_dict = {}

for col in cat_cols:
  #calling LabelEncoder
  le = LabelEncoder()

  # Train on training set
  X_train[col] = le.fit_transform(X_train[col])

  # Apply same mapping on validation set
  X_val[col] = le.transform(X_val[col])

  # Apply same mapping on test set
  X_test[col] = le.transform(X_test[col])
  le_dict[col] = le
In [ ]:
print("Number of rows in train data =", X_train.shape[0])
print("Number of rows in validation data =", X_val.shape[0])
print("Number of rows in test data =", X_test.shape[0])

Number of rows in train data = 15288
Number of rows in validation data = 5096
Number of rows in test data = 5096

Model Building¶

Model evaluation criteria¶

Model can make wrong predictions as:

  • The model can predict an applicant as certified when actually the application is denied.
  • The model can predict as Denied for a Certified application.

Which case is more important?

  • Predicting a false positive(model predicts as approved. but applicantion is denied) result in wastage of resources.
  • Predicting a false negative (model predicted Denied but application is Certified) causes miss of oppurtunity for a potential applicant as well as company.

How to reduce this loss

  • This loss can be reduced with increased F1 score.

Let's define a function to output different metrics (including recall) on the train and test set and a function to show confusion matrix so that we do not have to use the same code repetitively while evaluating models.

In [ ]:
# defining a function to compute different metrics to check performance of a classification model built using sklearn
def model_performance_classification_sklearn(model, predictors, target):
    """
    Function to compute different metrics to check classification model performance

    model: classifier
    predictors: independent variables
    target: dependent variable
    """

    # predicting using the independent variables
    pred = model.predict(predictors)

    acc = accuracy_score(target, pred)  # to compute Accuracy
    recall = recall_score(target, pred)  # to compute Recall
    precision = precision_score(target, pred)  # to compute Precision
    f1 = f1_score(target, pred)  # to compute F1-score

    # creating a dataframe of metrics
    df_perf = pd.DataFrame(
        {"Accuracy": acc, "Recall": recall, "Precision": precision, "F1": f1,},
        index=[0],
    )

    return df_perf #returning performance of the model.

Initial Model Building¶

Model Building - Original Data¶

In [ ]:
models = []  # Empty list to store all the models

# Append DecisionTreeClassifier to model with random_state set and specified as balanced(this gives more weightage for minority class)
models.append(("dtree", DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=1, class_weight='balanced')))

# Append Random forest Classifier to model
models.append(("Random forest", RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=1, class_weight='balanced')))

# Append BaggingClassifier  Classifier to model
models.append(("Bagging", BaggingClassifier(estimator=DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=1, class_weight='balanced'), random_state=1)))

# Append GradientBoosting Classifier to model
models.append(("GBM", GradientBoostingClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=1)))

# Append AdaBoostClassifier to model
models.append(("Adaboost", AdaBoostClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=1)))

print("\nTraining Performance:\n")
for name, model in models:
    model.fit(X_train, y_train) # Fitting model on X_train and y_train
    scores = f1_score(y_train, model.predict(X_train)) # Getting F1 score on train data
    print("{}: {:.3f}".format(name, scores)) # Print the scores with 2 decimal places

print('-' * 80)

print("\nValidation Performance:\n")
for name, model in models:
    model.fit(X_train, y_train) #Fitting on model on train data
    scores_val = f1_score(y_val, model.predict(X_val)) #F1 score for validation data
    print("{}: {:.3f}".format(name, scores_val)) # Print the scores with 2 decimal places

Training Performance:

dtree: 1.000
Random forest: 1.000
Bagging: 0.983
GBM: 0.826
Adaboost: 0.816
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Validation Performance:

dtree: 0.744
Random forest: 0.766
Bagging: 0.764
GBM: 0.822
Adaboost: 0.815
In [ ]:
print("\nTraining and Validation Performance Difference:\n")

for name, model in models:
    model.fit(X_train, y_train) # Fit the model ontrain data
    scores_train = f1_score(y_train, model.predict(X_train)) # Get f1 score on train data
    scores_val = f1_score(y_val, model.predict(X_val)) # Get f1 score on validation data
    difference = scores_train - scores_val # Get difference between train and validation metrics
    print("{}:        Training Score: {:.3f},      Validation Score: {:.4f},       Difference: {:.4f}".format(name, scores_train, scores_val, difference))

Training and Validation Performance Difference:

dtree:        Training Score: 1.000,      Validation Score: 0.7439,       Difference: 0.2561
Random forest:        Training Score: 1.000,      Validation Score: 0.7663,       Difference: 0.2337
Bagging:        Training Score: 0.983,      Validation Score: 0.7638,       Difference: 0.2196
GBM:        Training Score: 0.826,      Validation Score: 0.8218,       Difference: 0.0040
Adaboost:        Training Score: 0.816,      Validation Score: 0.8152,       Difference: 0.0008
  • AdaBoost has the best performance followed by GBM model as per the validation performance on actual data.

Oversampling¶

As the given data set is kind of imbalance data, we may get beter predictions if we over sample the data

In [ ]:
print("Before Oversampling, counts of label 'Denied': {} \n".format(sum(y_train == 0)))

sm = SMOTE(
    sampling_strategy=1, k_neighbors=5, random_state=1
)  # Synthetic Minority Over Sampling Technique for over sampling

# resample train data with over sampling
X_train_over, y_train_over = sm.fit_resample(X_train, y_train)


print("After Oversampling, counts of label 'Certified': {}".format(sum(y_train_over == 1)))
print("After Oversampling, counts of label 'Denied': {} \n".format(sum(y_train_over == 0)))


print("After Oversampling, the shape of train_X: {}".format(X_train_over.shape))
print("After Oversampling, the shape of train_y: {} \n".format(y_train_over.shape))

Before Oversampling, counts of label 'Denied': 5078 

After Oversampling, counts of label 'Certified': 10210
After Oversampling, counts of label 'Denied': 10210 

After Oversampling, the shape of train_X: (20420, 9)
After Oversampling, the shape of train_y: (20420,)
In [ ]:
models = []  # Empty list to store all the models

# Appending models into the list
models.append(("dtree", DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=1, class_weight='balanced')))
models.append(("Random forest", RandomForestClassifier(random_state=1, class_weight='balanced')))
models.append(("Bagging", BaggingClassifier(estimator=DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=1, class_weight='balanced'), random_state=1)))
models.append(("GBM", GradientBoostingClassifier(random_state=1)))
models.append(("Adaboost", AdaBoostClassifier(random_state=1)))

print("\n" "Training Performance:" "\n")
for name, model in models:
    model.fit(X_train_over, y_train_over) #fitting train data into model
    scores = f1_score(y_train_over, model.predict(X_train_over)) #get f1_score on training oversampled data
    print("{}: {:.3f}".format(name, scores)) # print scores

print('-' * 80)

print("\n" "Validation Performance:" "\n")

for name, model in models:
    model.fit(X_train_over, y_train_over) # Fitting Overfitting data to model
    scores = f1_score(y_val, model.predict(X_val)) #getting f1_Score on validation data
    print("{}: {:.3f}".format(name, scores)) #print scores

Training Performance:

Random forest: 1.000
Bagging: 0.978
GBM: 0.755
Adaboost: 0.727
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Validation Performance:

dtree: 0.717
Random forest: 0.733
Bagging: 0.730
GBM: 0.778
Adaboost: 0.749
In [ ]:
print("\nTraining and Validation Performance Difference:\n")

for name, model in models:
    model.fit(X_train_over, y_train_over) # Fit the model ontrain data
    scores_train = f1_score(y_train_over, model.predict(X_train_over)) # Get f1 score on train data
    scores_val = f1_score(y_val, model.predict(X_val)) # Get f1 score on validation data
    difference = scores_train - scores_val # Get difference between train and validation metrics
    print("{}:        Training Score: {:.3f},      Validation Score: {:.4f},       Difference: {:.4f}".format(name, scores_train, scores_val, difference))

Training and Validation Performance Difference:

dtree:        Training Score: 1.000,      Validation Score: 0.7174,       Difference: 0.2826
Random forest:        Training Score: 1.000,      Validation Score: 0.7333,       Difference: 0.2665
Bagging:        Training Score: 0.978,      Validation Score: 0.7299,       Difference: 0.2483
GBM:        Training Score: 0.755,      Validation Score: 0.7775,       Difference: -0.0229
Adaboost:        Training Score: 0.727,      Validation Score: 0.7494,       Difference: -0.0228
  • AdaBoost and GBM models are better performing on validation data when model tuned with oversampled data.

Model Building - Undersampled Data¶

In [ ]:
rus = RandomUnderSampler(random_state=1)  #using RandomUndersampler to undersample
X_train_un, y_train_un = rus.fit_resample(X_train, y_train) #creating undersample_train data
In [ ]:

print("After Under Sampling, the shape of train_X: {}".format(X_train_un.shape))

In [ ]:
models = []  # Empty list to store all the models

# Appending models into the list
models.append(("dtree", DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=1, class_weight='balanced')))
models.append(("Random forest", RandomForestClassifier(random_state=1, class_weight='balanced')))
models.append(("Bagging", BaggingClassifier(estimator=DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=1, class_weight='balanced'), random_state=1)))
models.append(("GBM", GradientBoostingClassifier(random_state=1)))
models.append(("Adaboost", AdaBoostClassifier(random_state=1)))

# Extracting performance numbers on undersample train data
print("\n" "Training Performance:" "\n")
for name, model in models:
    model.fit(X_train_un, y_train_un)
    scores = f1_score(y_train_un, model.predict(X_train_un))
    print("{}: {:.3f}".format(name, scores))
print('-' * 80)

print("\n" "Validation Performance:" "\n")
# Getting f1_score for validation data
for name, model in models:
    model.fit(X_train_un, y_train_un)
    scores = f1_score(y_val, model.predict(X_val))
    print("{}: {:.3f}".format(name, scores))

# To get difference of f1_score on both test and train data
for name, model in models:
    model.fit(X_train_un, y_train_un)
    scores_train = f1_score(y_train_un, model.predict(X_train_un))
    scores_val = f1_score(y_val, model.predict(X_val))
    difference3 = scores_train - scores_val
    print("{}: Training Score: {:.4f}, Validation Score: {:.4f}, Difference: {:.4f}".format(name, scores_train, scores_val, difference3))

Training and Validation Performance Difference:

dtree: Training Score: 1.0000, Validation Score: 0.6882, Difference: 0.3118
Random forest: Training Score: 0.9999, Validation Score: 0.7166, Difference: 0.2833
Bagging: Training Score: 0.9761, Validation Score: 0.6864, Difference: 0.2897
GBM: Training Score: 0.7222, Validation Score: 0.7689, Difference: -0.0467
Adaboost: Training Score: 0.7053, Validation Score: 0.7727, Difference: -0.0674
  • Out of all these 15 models, best performance is observed with Adaboost and GBM.
  • Adaboost and GBM performs best with oversampling.
  • Next better performance is seen with GBM undersampling.

Hyperparameter Tuning¶

Tuning AdaBoostClassifier model with Undersampled data¶

In [ ]:
# defining model
Model = AdaBoostClassifier(random_state=1)

# Parameter grid to pass in RandomSearchCV
param_grid = {
    "n_estimators": np.arange(10, 100, 10),
    "learning_rate": [0.1, 0.01, 0.2, 0.05, 1],
    "estimator": [
        DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=5, random_state=1),
        DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=7, random_state=1),
        DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=9, random_state=1),
    ],
}

# Type of scoring used to compare parameter combinations
scorer = metrics.make_scorer(metrics.f1_score)

#Calling RandomizedSearchCV
randomized_cv = RandomizedSearchCV(estimator=Model, param_distributions=param_grid, n_jobs = -1, n_iter=50, scoring=scorer, cv=5, random_state=1)

#Calling RandomizedSearchCV on under sampled data
randomized_cv.fit(X_train_un, y_train_un)

print("Best parameters are {} with CV score={}:" .format(randomized_cv.best_params_,randomized_cv.best_score_))

Best parameters are {'n_estimators': np.int64(50), 'learning_rate': 0.1, 'estimator': DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=5, random_state=1)} with CV score=0.7089255397645045:
In [ ]:
# configuring the best parameters
tuned_adb1 = AdaBoostClassifier(
    random_state=1,
    n_estimators=50,
    learning_rate=0.1,
    estimator=DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=5, random_state=1),
)
tuned_adb1.fit(X_train_un, y_train_un)
Out[ ]:

                                                    random_state=1),
                   learning_rate=0.1, random_state=1)

DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=5, random_state=1)
In [ ]:

adb_train1 = model_performance_classification_sklearn(tuned_adb1, X_train_un, y_train_un)
adb_train1

adb_val1

Tuning Gradient Boosting model with Undersampled Data¶

In [ ]:
#Creating pipeline
Model = GradientBoostingClassifier(random_state=1)

#Parameter grid to pass in RandomSearchCV
param_grid = {
    "init": [AdaBoostClassifier(random_state=1),DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=1)],
    "n_estimators": np.arange(125,175,25),
    "learning_rate": [0.01, 0.2, 0.05, 1],
    "subsample":[0.8,0.9,1],
    "max_features":[0.5,0.7,1],
}

# Type of scoring used to compare parameter combinations
scorer = metrics.make_scorer(metrics.f1_score)

#Calling RandomizedSearchCV
randomized_cv = RandomizedSearchCV(estimator=Model, param_distributions=param_grid, n_iter=50, scoring=scorer, cv=5, random_state=1, n_jobs = -1)

#Fitting parameters in RandomizedSearchCV
randomized_cv.fit(X_train_un,y_train_un)

print("Best parameters are {} with CV score={}:" .format(randomized_cv.best_params_,randomized_cv.best_score_))

Best parameters are {'subsample': 1, 'n_estimators': np.int64(125), 'max_features': 0.7, 'learning_rate': 0.05, 'init': AdaBoostClassifier(random_state=1)} with CV score=0.7105906991647967:
In [ ]:
tuned_gbm1 = GradientBoostingClassifier(
    random_state=1,
    subsample=1,
    n_estimators=125,
    max_features=0.7,
    learning_rate=0.05,
    init=AdaBoostClassifier(random_state=1),
)
tuned_gbm1.fit(X_train_un, y_train_un)

Tuning AdaBoost model with Oversampled data¶

In [ ]:
# defining model
Model = AdaBoostClassifier(random_state=1)

# Parameter grid to pass in RandomSearchCV
param_grid = {
    "n_estimators": np.arange(10, 100, 10),
    "learning_rate": [0.1, 0.01, 0.2, 0.05, 1],
    "estimator": [
        DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=5, random_state=1),
        DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=7, random_state=1),
        DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=9, random_state=1),
    ],
}

# Type of scoring used to compare parameter combinations
scorer = metrics.make_scorer(metrics.f1_score)

#Calling RandomizedSearchCV
randomized_cv = RandomizedSearchCV(estimator=Model, param_distributions=param_grid, n_jobs = -1, n_iter=50, scoring=scorer, cv=5, random_state=1)

#Fitting parameters in RandomizedSearchCV
randomized_cv.fit(X_train_over, y_train_over)

print("Best parameters are {} with CV score={}:" .format(randomized_cv.best_params_,randomized_cv.best_score_))

Best parameters are {'n_estimators': np.int64(40), 'learning_rate': 0.1, 'estimator': DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=7, random_state=1)} with CV score=0.7495476860096357:

Tuning GBM with over sampled data¶

In [ ]:
#Creating pipeline
Model = GradientBoostingClassifier(random_state=1)

#Parameter grid to pass in RandomSearchCV
param_grid = {
    "init": [AdaBoostClassifier(random_state=1),DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=1)],
    "n_estimators": np.arange(125,175,25),
    "learning_rate": [0.01, 0.2, 0.05, 1],
    "subsample":[0.8,0.9,1],
    "max_features":[0.5,0.7,1],
}

# Type of scoring used to compare parameter combinations
scorer = metrics.make_scorer(metrics.f1_score)

#Calling RandomizedSearchCV
randomized_cv = RandomizedSearchCV(estimator=Model, param_distributions=param_grid, n_iter=50, scoring=scorer, cv=5, random_state=1, n_jobs = -1)

#Fitting parameters in RandomizedSearchCV
randomized_cv.fit(X_train_over,y_train_over)

print("Best parameters are {} with CV score={}:" .format(randomized_cv.best_params_,randomized_cv.best_score_))

Best parameters are {'subsample': 0.9, 'n_estimators': np.int64(150), 'max_features': 0.7, 'learning_rate': 0.2, 'init': AdaBoostClassifier(random_state=1)} with CV score=0.7474804654342829:
In [ ]:
tuned_gbm2 = GradientBoostingClassifier(
    random_state=1,
    subsample=0.9,
    n_estimators=150,
    max_features=0.7,
    learning_rate=0.2,
    init=AdaBoostClassifier(random_state=1),
)
tuned_gbm2.fit(X_train_over, y_train_over)

    tuned_gbm2, X_train_over, y_train_over
)

Model Comparison and Final Model Selection¶

In [ ]:
# training performance comparison

models_train_comp_df = pd.concat(
    [
        gbm1_train.T,
        gbm2_train.T,
        adb_train1.T,
        adb_train2.T,
    ],
    axis=1,
)
models_train_comp_df.columns = [
    "Gradient boosting trained with Undersampled data",
    "Gradient boosting trained with Oversampled data",
    "AdaBoost trained with Undersampled data",
    "AdaBoost trained with Oversampled data",
]
print("Training performance comparison:")
models_train_comp_df

Training performance comparison:
Out[ ]:
Gradient boosting trained with Undersampled data Gradient boosting trained with Oversampled data AdaBoost trained with Undersampled data AdaBoost trained with Oversampled data
Accuracy 0.712 0.766 0.706 0.708
Recall 0.741 0.776 0.722 0.754
Precision 0.701 0.761 0.700 0.797
F1 0.720 0.768 0.711 0.775
In [ ]:
# Validation performance comparison

models_train_comp_df = pd.concat(
    [ gbm1_val.T, gbm2_val.T, adb_val1.T, adb_val2.T], axis=1,
)
models_train_comp_df.columns = [
    "Gradient boosting trained with Undersampled data",
    "Gradient boosting trained with Oversampled data",
    "AdaBoost trained with Undersampled data",
    "AdaBoost trained with Oversampled data",
]
print("Validation performance comparison:")
models_train_comp_df

Validation performance comparison:
Out[ ]:
Gradient boosting trained with Undersampled data Gradient boosting trained with Oversampled data AdaBoost trained with Undersampled data AdaBoost trained with Oversampled data
Accuracy 0.716 0.709 0.710 0.708
Recall 0.732 0.764 0.718 0.754
Precision 0.823 0.793 0.825 0.797
F1 0.775 0.778 0.768 0.775
  • From the above comparision of validation test results we see, f1_score is better for AdaBoost trained on Oversampled data.
  • Let us apply test data on ada_oversampled model
In [ ]:
# Let's check the performance on test set
ada_test = model_performance_classification_sklearn(tuned_adb_over, X_test, y_test)
ada_test
Out[ ]:
Accuracy Recall Precision F1
0 0.699 0.760 0.783 0.771
  • On test data, the AdaBoost oversampling results are mentioned above.
  • We can see a decent performance od the model on test data. Around 77%
  • This performance is in line with what we achieved with this model on the train and validation sets
  • So, this is a generalized model
  • The confidance score of this will be in the range of 72-82 percent
  • This can still further be tuned for better performance.

Feature importance¶

In [ ]:
# Get feature importances from the tuned AdaBoost model trained on oversampled data
importances = tuned_adb_over.feature_importances_

# Get the feature names from the training data
feature_names = X_train_over.columns

# Sort feature importances and get the corresponding indices
indices = np.argsort(importances)

# Plot the feature importances
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
plt.title("Feature Importances (AdaBoost with Oversampling)")
plt.barh(range(len(indices)), importances[indices], color="violet", align="center")
plt.yticks(range(len(indices)), [feature_names[i] for i in indices])
plt.xlabel("Relative Importance")
plt.show()
  • From feature importance graph, we see that education_of_employee is an important feature that majorly decides VISA approval.
  • The next important feature are 'has_job_experience' and 'yearly_wage' followed by 'region_of_employment'

Bussiness Insights and Recommendations¶

  • Applicants with higher education are more likely to be certified. Education_of_employee is a key factor contributing for Visa Approval.
  • Although eduaction_of_employee is key factor, not all applications with higher educationa are approved visa. Other factors like has_job_experience, yearly_wage, region_of_employement decide the actual appoval.
  • As the number of final approvals will be fixed, it is advicable to first short list the Doctorate eduaction applicants.
  • This also ensures that highly qualified applicatant will be processed and hence the chances of approval increases.
  • Combination of educational qualification and has job experience can be used as indicators for approval in most of the cases.
